content: hey so I'm working on an assignment where I need to add NVIDIA DPU support to the OPI DPU operator (it's a k8s operator, currently only supports Intel and Marvell). NVIDIA already has their own DPF operator (DOCA Platform Framework) that does BFB flashing + DOCA/OVS offload config for BlueField-3. I don't want to reimplement any of that, I just want to bridge the two. Can you help me think through the actual integration pattern before I start writing anything? I'm thinking either some kind of adapter or maybe a sub-operator that translates CRDs, not sure which is actually better

Good instinct to separate this into "which pattern" before writing code. Let me lay out three real options:

1. Reimplement DPF's provisioning logic directly inside OPI's controller. Downside: you now own keeping up with every DOCA/BFB firmware change NVIDIA ships, forever, for a stack you didn't build.
2. In-process adapter that imports DPF's Go client library directly and calls it from inside OPI's controller. No new CRDs needed, but this hard-couples OPI's go.mod to DPF's release cadence, and a panic in DPF's client code runs in the same process as your controller - it can take the whole thing down.
3. Sub-operator with a CRD translation layer: a small new controller inside OPI watches your existing Dpu CR, and when vendor==nvidia it creates/updates DPF's own CRDs (BFB, DPUSet) through the Kubernetes API using an unstructured/dynamic client - so you never actually import DPF's Go module at compile time, you just need to know the GVKs and field names.

Option 3 is the one I'd lean towards for exactly the reuse goal you described. It's the same basic idea as how Cluster API's infrastructure providers work - a small translation controller, everything talks through the API server, no shared binary. DPF stays completely untouched and can be upgraded on its own schedule.

ok yeah 3 makes sense, going with that. one thing I'm not sure about - if I use unstructured.Unstructured for the DPF objects instead of importing their real types, is that actually a normal thing to do or is that a hack? just want to make sure this isn't a weird choice before I put it in my design doc

It's a legitimate and pretty common pattern, not a hack. Using unstructured.Unstructured with an explicit GroupVersionKind is the standard way controller-runtime clients talk to CRDs owned by a completely separate project when you don't want a compile-time dependency on that project's API package. You lose some compile-time type safety on the DPF side (you're keying into fields by string path instead of a typed struct), but you gain the ability to upgrade OPI and DPF independently, which is exactly the trade-off you want here. If you ever did want typed access later, you could still generate a thin client from DPF's CRD schema without changing this overall architecture.

cool that's what I figured. can you give me a mermaid sequence diagram for the create/provisioning flow - starting from kubectl apply on the OPI side all the way down to the actual BlueField hardware and back up to status. and a second one for deletion, I specifically want the finalizer/ownership handling shown correctly so kubectl delete on the OPI side actually garbage collects the DPF CRs instead of leaving them orphaned

Creation flow: user applies DpuOperatorConfig with vendor: nvidia -> API server notifies the Core OPI Controller -> Core Controller checks spec.vendor and hands off to your new NVIDIA Adapter Controller -> Adapter translates the OPI spec into BFB and DPUSet objects and creates them with an ownerReference pointing back at the Dpu resource -> API server notifies DPF Operator (unmodified) -> DPF flashes the BFB and configures OVS-DOCA/vDPA on the actual hardware -> DPF writes status back onto DPUSet -> Adapter watches that status change, turns it into a simplified phase, and writes it onto the OPI Dpu status -> user sees Ready from kubectl get.

Deletion flow: user deletes the Dpu resource -> your OPI-side finalizer triggers a reconcile in the Adapter Controller -> Adapter deletes the owned BFB/DPUSet CRs -> DPF's own finalizer on DPUSet runs its hardware deprovisioning/reset logic first, then removes its own finalizer -> once the DPUSet object is actually gone, the Adapter removes the OPI-side finalizer -> the Dpu resource is finally deleted. The key part you asked about is that the OPI finalizer has to wait for DPF's own finalizer to finish first, otherwise you'd garbage collect the Dpu resource while DPF is still mid-teardown on real hardware.

(full mermaid source is in architecture_design.md, sections 7 and 8)

last thing - can you rough out a go skeleton for this adapter controller. controller-runtime style (Reconciler struct + SetupWithManager), unstructured client like we talked about, needs to build a BFB and DPUSet object from the OPI spec, set the owner ref, and have some kind of status aggregation stubbed out. doesn't need to actually compile against a real cluster, just needs to be structurally right so I can build off it later. also please don't reference some fake external package for the OPI types, just define a minimal version of them in the same file so the file is actually self contained

[code artifact: feature_skeleton.go] Everything is kept in one self-contained package (no import of a nonexistent opi-operator API package): a minimal Dpu/DpuSpec/DpuStatus type definition at the top standing in for the real OPI API, GVK constants for DPF's BFB and DPUSet CRDs, an NvidiaAdapterReconciler with a Reconcile method that builds unstructured BFB/DPUSet objects from the Dpu spec, sets the owner reference via controllerutil.SetControllerReference, does create-or-update, then reads DPUSet.status back to aggregate a Pending/Provisioning/Ready/Failed phase, plus the finalizer-aware delete path and a SetupWithManager wiring watches on the owned DPF kind. Left TODO comments on the couple of DPF field names (bfbVersion, docaProfile, status.ready) that would need confirming against the actual pinned DPF CRD schema before this touches a real cluster. Full source saved as feature_skeleton.go alongside this transcript.
